primary_diagnosis.name: Acute decompensated hydrocephalus with acutely raised intracranial pressure and impending cerebral herniation

comorbidities.name: Macrocephaly / abnormally rapid head circumference growth under investigation

pathophysiology_summary: Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) production, circulation or absorption is obstructed, causing progressive ventricular dilation and a rising intracranial volume. In an infant with open sutures the head enlarges and the fontanelle tenses, but once compensatory capacity is exceeded the intracranial pressure rises steeply. The clinical tetrad here — rapidly increasing head circumference, a tense bulging anterior fontanelle, sunsetting eyes (dorsal midbrain/tectal-plate compression producing an upward-gaze palsy), and central (projectile, non-bilious) vomiting — reflects acutely raised ICP. The relative bradycardia, systolic hypertension and irregular respiration (Cushing's triad), pallor and fluctuating consciousness signal brainstem compression and impending cerebral/tonsillar herniation, which is rapidly fatal if the pressure is not relieved. Definitive management is emergent neuroimaging (CT/MRI) and urgent CSF diversion (external ventricular drain or shunt). This is a time-critical neurosurgical emergency.

differential.evidence_basis: Inconsolable high-pitched cry, forceful vomiting and a bulging soft spot in an infant with a known rapidly enlarging head point to raised ICP.
Vomiting in an infant is common — but non-bilious PROJECTILE vomiting with neuro signs and a tense fontanelle is not simple gastroenteritis.
Colic does not cause a tense non-softening fontanelle, sunsetting eyes, projectile vomiting or a fluctuating conscious level.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The under-triage trap is anchoring on 'vomiting baby' as gastroenteritis/reflux/colic. Central vomiting plus neuro signs must escalate the concern, not reassure.

differential.evidence_basis: Full ICP tetrad present (enlarging head, tense fontanelle, sunsetting eyes, projectile vomiting) plus declining consciousness — a neurosurgical emergency.

Also causes a bulging fontanelle, irritability and vomiting in an infant; afebrile here and the mechanical macrocephaly signs favour raised ICP, but meningitis cannot be excluded remotely and needs the same emergency pathway.

differential.diagnosis: non-accidental head injury / abusive head trauma
differential.evidence_basis: Can present with a bulging fontanelle, vomiting and reduced consciousness in an infant; screened for at ED, not excludable via chat.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Whichever of these it is, the disposition is identical: emergency ambulance to a paediatric ED with neurosurgery. Escalation must not wait on distinguishing them.

differential.diagnosis: acute decompensated hydrocephalus with raised ICP and impending herniation
differential.evidence_basis: Classic complete ICP tetrad with Cushing's-triad physiology and fluctuating consciousness in an infant with known rapidly increasing head circumference — requires emergent imaging and CSF diversion.

red_flags.description: Signs of acutely raised intracranial pressure — tense non-softening bulging fontanelle, sunsetting eyes and forceful non-bilious projectile (central) vomiting
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates critically raised ICP; can progress to brainstem herniation within minutes to hours.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Fatal cerebral/tonsillar herniation or irreversible brain injury from delayed CSF diversion.

red_flags.description: Altered / fluctuating level of consciousness in an infant — progressive lethargy, pallor and difficulty rousing between crying spells
red_flags.clinical_significance: A declining conscious level is a late and ominous sign of raised ICP and impending herniation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to coma and respiratory compromise if not escalated immediately.

red_flags.description: Continuous uncharacteristic high-pitched 'cerebral' cry in an infant
red_flags.clinical_significance: A high-pitched cry is a recognised sign of raised ICP / neurological distress in infants.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Misattributing the cry to colic delays recognition of a neurosurgical emergency.

red_flags.description: Cushing's triad — systolic hypertension, relative bradycardia and irregular respiration (clinician-measured, not obtainable via telehealth)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Cushing's triad signals brainstem compression and imminent herniation — a pre-terminal sign.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Herniation and death; in telehealth its absence from view must NOT be taken as reassurance.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating the vomiting as a tummy bug, reflux or overfeeding. Central, projectile, non-bilious vomiting with neuro signs and a tense fontanelle is not gastrointestinal — it is raised ICP until proven otherwise.
Attributing the inconsolable high-pitched cry to colic. Colic never produces a tense non-softening fontanelle, sunsetting eyes or a fluctuating conscious level.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Diagnostic overshadowing of the known head-growth workup
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Assuming the new symptoms are just part of the ongoing macrocephaly investigation and can wait for the next appointment. The new acute decompensation is the emergency.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: False reassurance from a normal-looking video
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being reassured by an infant who briefly settles or looks okay on camera; the fluctuating course and the inability to measure ICP or vitals remotely mean the safest read is emergency escalation.

Attempting to manage or observe at home. Telehealth cannot palpate the fontanelle, measure vitals or image the brain — the only correct action is immediate 000 with carer-deliverable holding measures.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Head circumference percentile and suture separation on examination
Pupillary responses and formal upward-gaze palsy assessment
Blood pressure, heart rate and respiratory pattern (Cushing's triad)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Immediate 000 ambulance for emergency transfer to a paediatric emergency department with neurosurgery; while waiting, keep the infant with the head elevated to about 30 degrees, keep him nil by mouth, do not lay him flat, and unlock the door for paramedics.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the emergency itself (the carer-observable constellation is unambiguous); the real risk is UNDER-action — under-triaging to reflux/gastroenteritis/colic or deferring to the existing head-growth appointment instead of calling 000.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RCH Melbourne CPG — Raised intracranial pressure / CSF shunt malfunction
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Infant signs of raised ICP (bulging fontanelle, sunsetting eyes, vomiting, altered consciousness) require emergency neurosurgical assessment and urgent neuroimaging.
Suspected impending herniation / Cushing's physiology is a time-critical emergency mandating immediate ambulance transfer; head-up positioning and airway protection are supportive measures.
